imageAlt: A US Marine M48 Patton tank advances through rubble to support 1st Battalion 5th Marines inside the Citadel of Hue, February 1968
imageCaption: A US Marine M48 tank advances through the Citadel of Hue to support 1st Battalion, 5th Marines, February 1968. US Marine Corps / National Archives / Public Domain.

longForm.title: Street by Street: The Battle of Hue City, January–March 1968
longForm.excerpt: On the morning of January 31, 1968, NVA and Viet Cong forces seized Hue — Vietnam's ancient imperial capital. It took US Marines and ARVN soldiers 26 days to take it back.
paragraphs: In the early hours of January 31, 1968, while a truce was nominally in effect for the Vietnamese Lunar New Year — Tet — approximately 7,500 North Vietnamese Army and Viet Cong troops moved into Hue, South Vietnam's third-largest city and its ancient imperial capital. By dawn, they held most of it. The speed and scale of the attack shattered assumptions that had governed American strategy for two years: that the enemy lacked the strength for a conventional urban offensive, that the population would not tolerate a Communist seizure, and that the war was, in some meaningful sense, being won. All three assumptions were wrong.

Hue had a particular significance that made it both a symbolically compelling target and a militarily treacherous one. The Citadel — a 19th-century fortified imperial palace complex in the northern part of the city — offered the NVA natural defensive terrain: thick masonry walls, interconnected courtyards, and structures that resisted artillery and air strikes without collapsing in ways that cleared fields of fire. The NVA flag flew from the Citadel's main flagpole for most of the battle, a visible propaganda asset that American and South Vietnamese commanders understood they could not leave in place. Getting it down required fighting through the walls.

The initial American response was hampered by a failure of situational awareness that historians have since documented in detail. Brigadier General Foster LaHue, commanding Task Force X-Ray at Phu Bai, ordered a company of Marines — Alpha Company, 1st Battalion, 1st Marines under Captain Gordon Batcheller — to drive into Hue on 31 January and relieve the MACV compound, a small US military advisory facility still holding out south of the Perfume River. LaHue believed he was sending a company to deal with a minor incursion. Batcheller's Marines drove into an ambush. Four Marines were killed in the first hour. The company fought its way into the MACV compound and could go no further.

It took several days before American commanders acknowledged the scale of what they were facing and began committing the forces required. Lieutenant Colonel Ernest Cheatham, commanding 2nd Battalion, 5th Marines, arrived with the first substantial reinforcement. Cheatham's battalion would bear the heaviest fighting in the New City south of the river, clearing block by block with rifles, machine guns, and eventually — after permission to use heavy weapons was finally granted — M48 tanks, recoilless rifles, and 106mm guns firing directly into building facades. Standard American doctrine, built around mobility and firepower, was nearly useless in an urban environment where every intersection was a kill zone and every building a fortified position. The Marines had to relearn tactics their predecessors had practiced in Manila in 1945.

South of the river fell to Cheatham's Marines over roughly two weeks. The Citadel was harder. The ARVN 1st Division, headquartered inside the Citadel, had held a small perimeter from the first night. Recapturing the rest required coordination between ARVN airborne and infantry battalions and elements of the US 1st Battalion, 5th Marines, which entered the Citadel in the second week of February. Inside the walls, fighting was room by room. The NVA had prepared defences for exactly this kind of battle — spider holes, firing positions cut through walls, pre-registered mortar coordinates. American air support was restricted for much of the battle to avoid destroying the cultural heritage structures; when restrictions were eventually lifted, the bombing further reduced streets already reduced to rubble.

The Hue massacre — the systematic execution of civilians and captured South Vietnamese government and military personnel by NVA and Viet Cong forces during their 26-day occupation — became one of the most documented atrocities of the Vietnam War. Mass graves discovered after the battle contained at least 2,800 bodies; subsequent discoveries raised the figure to between 2,800 and 5,700, depending on the accounting methodology. Victims included civil servants, teachers, Catholic priests and foreign clergy, and anyone associated with the Saigon government. The killings were not incidental: recovered NVA documents from the period of the occupation indicated systematic targeting of designated class enemies. The massacre received far less contemporary coverage in the United States than the My Lai incident, which occurred two months later.

The Citadel was declared secure on 25 February 1968. Isolated fighting continued for another week; the battle is conventionally dated as ending 3 March. American casualties: 216 Marines and soldiers killed, 1,584 wounded. ARVN casualties: approximately 452 killed. NVA and Viet Cong killed: estimated at 5,000, though communist sources have never confirmed a reliable figure. The city itself was devastated. An estimated 80 percent of Hue's structures were damaged or destroyed.

The battle's significance was not primarily tactical. Militarily, Hue was retaken, and the Tet Offensive as a whole was a military defeat for North Vietnam: the expected popular uprising in the South did not materialise, and NVA and VC losses were severe. But the image of a major South Vietnamese city held by the enemy for nearly a month, and the cost of retaking it, destroyed the credibility of official American optimism about the war's progress. The Military Assistance Command Vietnam had spent months briefing journalists and senators on progress indicators. Hue demonstrated what those indicators had concealed.

The historiography of Hue has been complicated by the same polarisations that define American memory of the Vietnam War. James Willbanks's 2004 operational history and Eric Hammel's earlier battlefield account provide the most detailed tactical reconstructions. Nick Turse's later work, focusing on civilian casualties across the war, situates Hue within a pattern of atrocity that American historians have been slower to document than Vietnamese ones. What the documentary record makes clear, regardless of interpretive frame, is that the battle exposed a gap between the war American commanders believed they were fighting and the war they were actually in — a gap that would not be closed before the war ended.

sources.title: Hue 1968: A Turning Point of the American War in Vietnam

shortForm.title: One Hundred and Eight Against Two Thousand: The Battle of Long Tan, August 18, 1966
shortForm.excerpt: D Company, 6th RAR — 108 Australians — spent three and a half hours in a rubber plantation in Phuoc Tuy Province fighting an estimated 2,500 NVA and Viet Cong. Eighteen Australians were killed. The enemy lost at least 245.
paragraphs: The Battle of Long Tan grew out of a routine patrol. On the afternoon of 18 August 1966, D Company, 6th Battalion, Royal Australian Regiment was following up a mortar attack on Nui Dat base in Phuoc Tuy Province when it made contact with what appeared to be a small enemy force in the rubber plantation near the village of Long Tan. What Major Harry Smith's 108-man company had walked into was elements of the NVA 275th Regiment and the Viet Cong D445 Provincial Mobile Battalion — an estimated 2,500 men. The Australians were encircled within the hour.

What followed was three and a half hours of close-range combat in driving monsoon rain that reduced visibility to metres. Smith's company held its perimeter through fire superiority and artillery support from Nui Dat — 1,492 rounds fired by New Zealand and Australian gunners over the course of the engagement. When the ammunition ran low, a South Vietnamese Air Force Caribou flew through heavy fire to drop resupply by parachute. The relief force — Armoured Personnel Carriers of A Squadron, 3rd Cavalry Regiment — reached D Company's position just as darkness fell. The NVA withdrew, leaving their dead behind.

Eighteen Australians were killed and twenty-four wounded. Estimates of NVA and Viet Cong dead ranged from 245 confirmed bodies found on the field to over 500 by subsequent intelligence assessments. The ratio of casualties and the decision to hold rather than withdraw despite encirclement defined Long Tan as a significant tactical victory for Australian forces. Harry Smith was awarded the Military Cross. Three Victoria Crosses were awarded to men of D Company: Warrant Officer Kevin Wheatley, Private Ray Simpson, and Private Peter Badcoe — though Badcoe's VC was for a separate action.

Long Tan is remembered in Australia as that country's defining battle of the Vietnam War — its Gallipoli-equivalent in terms of cultural memory, though the parallel obscures more than it reveals. Gallipoli was a defeat. Long Tan was a victory, achieved by a company that refused to break under conditions that would have justified withdrawal. The Australian War Memorial holds the unit's battle ensign, a Vietnamese-made flag of D Company retrieved from the battlefield — one of the more unusual primary artefacts of the campaign.

sources.title: Long Tan: The Australian Vietnam War Battle

onThisDay.headline: Hamburg Surrenders to British 7th Armoured Division, Ending Combat in Germany's Second City
onThisDay.body: On 3 May 1945, Hamburg — Germany's second-largest city and its principal Atlantic port — surrendered to the British 7th Armoured Division, the 'Desert Rats.' The city had been devastated by the RAF bombing campaign of July 1943, Operation GOMORRAH, which killed an estimated 37,000 civilians in a firestorm. By May 1945, resistance in the city was nominal; the Wehrmacht garrison had largely dissolved. The formal surrender ended organised combat in northern Germany five days before the general capitulation of 8 May.
